notes.anchor: the young man, or rather the boy, Caesar
notes.note: Octavian, Caesar's nineteen-year-old great-nephew and testamentary heir, who had raised a private army of Caesar's veterans against Antony on his own initiative in late 44 BC. "Caesar" here is the heir, not the murdered dictator.

notes.anchor: the Martian Legion, the Fourth Legion, which judged their own consul a public enemy
notes.note: Two veteran legions that defected from the consul Antony to Octavian and the Republic in late 44 BC. Their "own consul" declared a public enemy (hostis) is Mark Antony, consul of 44.

notes.anchor: first by the Ides of March
notes.note: The Ides of March (15 March 44 BC), the day Decimus Brutus and the other Liberators assassinated Caesar; Cicero means that Brutus committed himself irrevocably by that act.
